speaker: interviewer
text: Today is July 23, 1996. We are interviewing Eva Safferman Zysman. My name is Barbara Johnson. We're in Fair Lawn, New Jersey, United States of America. The language of the interview will be English. My name is Barbara Johnson. Today is July 23, 1996. I'm conducting an interview of survivor Eva Zysman Safferman. We're in Fair Lawn, New Jersey, United States of America, and the language will be English. Spelling of the names are Johnson, J-O-H-N-S-O-N, Safferman, S-A-F-F-E-R-M-A-N. Tell me your name, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Eva Safferman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zysman, Z-Y-S-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I live in Fair Lawn, New Jersey, 7 Sandow Court.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what day of the week?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lodz, Poland, L-O-D-Z, Poland, P-O-L-A-N-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zachary. I have to spell it?

speaker: interviewer
text: If you'd like. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. You would like it? Z-A-C-H-A-R-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was an accountant and a businessman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he own a business?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of business?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was some kind of clothing place, manufacturing plant.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was men's clothing. Yeah, I think pants or something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he do in his accounting business? What kind of people did he service?

speaker: interviewee
text: He service a very, very large company, which was doing the tar for the roofs in Poland. It's one of the biggest company. I think it was [? Oppenheimer ?] was the name of the company.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a Polish company?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the owners were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was your father born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe he was born in 1903. I don't remember the month.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was the only child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what did your father like to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I really don't remember that well. I was kind of young. But he used to take me out. He used to bring me stuff, food, all the best things. When they weren't available to everybody else, I used to have them.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that, that they weren't available?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was already when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your house like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a very nice apartment with a terrace. It was in a building that my grandfather owned, which my father really financed. And we had a very nice apartment-- two bedrooms and kitchen. We were few of the lucky ones. They had a bathroom in the house. Not too many people in Lodz had it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the street that you lived on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poludnowa 94, P-O-L-U-D-N-O-W-A, 94.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Anna. Before the war they called her [? Hanaka, ?] [? Hanaka ?] Zysman, born Berman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother was born in, I believe, in December 1998.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a very pretty woman, dark hair, very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she do?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a homemaker. She was home. She's taking care of the three of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did she do that? Did she cook wonderful things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she had a maid, but she did the cooking.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sorts of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for Saturday, she would make gefilte fish. She would cook, you know, chicken soup, traditional Jewish food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maid, or her-- or the maid--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that was in the house--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --did she live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She used to come in in the morning and leave at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't remember. I think it was [? Marisa. ?] I'm not sure, but I believe so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have one maid for as long as you can remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember-- yeah. She used to tell me stories and used to sing to me. And then I used to get scared sometimes because she used to tell me those scary stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, at that time I was maybe eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have any hobbies or interests?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was-- she liked to embroider. She liked to make beautiful pictures. The house was full of embroidered stuff that she made-- pictures, tablecloth. She was very, very talented this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she do them?

speaker: interviewee
text: By hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she draw a picture first or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't recall. But sometimes they-- you buy already made, and then she would just embroider them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religiously observant?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents were very, very religious. My father was not as religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother, she-- she had to do whatever, you know, her husband. She-- she had a kosher home, but she-- she wasn't religious. My father was the one that was a little bit lax, because sometimes he had to go into work on a Saturday. And of course, I was very heartbroken because I didn't like that. All my friends were very religious. Sometimes I used to cry because my father turned on the light on Saturday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have many friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I had friends, children that lived in the same house, same building. We used to play together. I used to have a bicycle. I used to let them ride on my bicycle, because this was kind of luxury before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: The city in which you lived was a large city.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a large industrial city. Like New York is in the United States, that was Lodz was in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to public school on [NON-ENGLISH] 18. And then the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what grade were you when the war did break out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe I was in the sixth grade, I-- because I think I skipped. I-- I don't remember really.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sorts of things did you study in school? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Arithmetic, Polish, spelling, geography. Was-- was kind of long time ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: You speak other languages now. Did you learn them in school or afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Poland, no, afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have religious training?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother took a rebbetzin for me to give me some instructions how to read, but I wasn't too good in it. I used to memorize one story. Because the rebbetzin used to tell-- give me a-- to read a-- a love story she had in the back of her book, and I learned one by heart. And every time she asked me, and I wanted to read this one, till she got wise to me. She says, read something else, and I couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family take vacations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. I used to go with my mom in a spa before the war. We went to [? Sczcawnica, ?] which was a-- a resort in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it? On the ocean?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- in the mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the ocean--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --silly question.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, it was in the mountains. It was beautiful there. I was a little girl. I remember they used to have, like, a goat, and they used to milk the goat and I would drink the goat milk. It was really nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told me about a camp that you went to with your aunt one summer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was-- I was a very poor eater and, like, always so green. I didn't want to eat. So my mom decided that she send me away with my aunt, which was my Aunt [? Manucia, ?] to a camp so I should learn how to eat. And I did go to the camp the-- the same year that the war broke out. I did learn how to eat. But unfortunately, when I got back there wasn't too much to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they teach you how to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, just being with other children and seeing that they eat, and you know, it was different. At home I was by myself, and I-- you know. Please, swallow-- my mother used to run after me with a spoon. But over there with other children I just really learned how to eat, and I guess I was getting older, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have pretty clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. I had dresses. I had patent leather shoes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your favorite dress?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Honestly, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you came back from camp, and the war broke out. What sorts of things let you know that there was a war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was-- first of all, we had to put on a Jewish star. We weren't supposed to be in the street after a certain hour. We were not supposed to go all around, you know? We just had to be stayed wherever we were. This was still in Lodz, not in the ghetto yet. But of course, already things were getting tough. You couldn't get food as much as you-- you wanted to. And my father used to be a good provider, but still there wasn't too much to be gotten, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear about events?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we heard it. I mean, people were talking, and my father used to read the paper, and we had the radio. And you heard what happened, because the-- the war broke out, and in three days we were already occupied by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father allowed to have this radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was not. This was against the law. If they would found it, they would have probably executed him or punished him in some way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was family life any different at this stage than it had been?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mom already took all the jewelry and started hiding it in the stove. He-- she took out a brick from the stove and took all her jewelry, because she had quite a lot that my father gave her, and she used to hide it. Every time, you know, she heard somebody might be coming, she used to hide it away. So it was kind of very unsettling.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of jewelry did she have? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. She had-- she had a diamond ring. She had a beautiful diamond watch that he bought her once for her anniversary. And he even bought a diamond ring for me. He says I should have that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you'd hear people coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German people. They used to come to the houses, and they used to inspect the houses. And then one day, we-- we heard a big commotion outside, and there was a lot of Germans. They came into the house. And my mother heard it too. They went to my grandfather's house, and they took everybody out-- my grandmother, my grandfather, all the aunts and uncles-- and they shipped them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when my mother saw that, she went into the house, and she started packing all this-- a little stuff that we had. She put some in valises. And we were staying by the door when the German officer came in, and he asked my mother, what are you doing here? So she says, well, I see that you taken away my-- my in-laws and all my brother and sister-in-laws. Then I'm ready to go too. So he comes over and looks at me and then says, oh, boy, is she Jewish? And my mother says, yes, she's Jewish. So he says, you go back. You're not going anyplace. So then we still-- we-- we remained in our apartment. But all my aunt-- my-- all my aunts and uncles and my grandmother and grandfather was evacuated. Did you ever see them again?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we never-- no. We heard that they were taken to [NON-ENGLISH], and I think they were executed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did the German officer, soldier, ask if you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess because I didn't look Jewish. I had blue eyes, and I had red hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, next we just unpacked everything again. My mother, before we would-- ready to-- she left the window open because my father wasn't home, and so this way he could come back and take out the stuff that she hid in the stove. And then so we-- we went back in, and we just stood on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have cousins who were evacuated as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. At that time, no. No, I didn't have any cousins. I had one cousin, which this cousin left for the United States before the war. And of course, other cousins, there were some were in Israel and some were in a different town. They did not live-- all of them did not live in-- in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the next thing that happened to your family in terms of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next time, there was-- we were supposed to leave our home, and we were supposed to go into the ghetto. So that time, my father came back, and we packed everything, whatever little we could, and we marched with everybody else into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where your father had been? You say he came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was doing business. He was still doing business during the war. He would take off the Jewish star, and he would go and do-- conduct business.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did this Jewish star look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a yellow-- like a Magen David, like a Jewish star. And inside there was written in black, Jude, what means Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were these stars supplied to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You-- you-- you bought them, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to buy them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how much they cost?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I can't remember now. I really don't remember that much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they sewn onto your clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we had to sew it on ourselves. So then we all marched to the ghetto, and my father got us an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take things with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we took a lot of things with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Such as?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't take too much. Some clothes, some bedding, a couple of pots, I guess, pans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember, but maybe we did. I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you march alone or with other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, other people marched too. Everybody marched toward the ghetto. And there was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there people directing the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: No we just walked by ourselves. It was a part of the Lodz that I was never before there. They-- this part was like poor part of Lodz. So we went there. We got an apartment. It was one big room and a kitchen. And I had a grandmother, which was in a nursing home at that time in Poland. So my mother took the-- her mother out and she lived with us. And of course, was the great hunger. We had very little to eat. There was rationed-- everything was rationed, like would get one ounce of kohlrabi and-- and maybe bread for a whole family for the whole week. And-- but she stayed with us, till one day my father brought some horse meat that he got, because he worked for the-- for the-- he was an accountant in the ghetto [NON-ENGLISH]. So he was able to get this. And when she saw this and she said, my dear children, I can't stay with you anymore, because I cannot stay and eat this non-kosher food. And the nursing home was still in existence, so my father had to take her back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why had she been in a nursing home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my grandfather, when he passed on, he said to her, I don't want you to be-- you have eight children, but I never want you to be with any of them. And he felt that she would be better off being in a nursing home. And she was very happy there. I remember even before the war, I used to-- right into the war-- in the war in the beginning, I used to bring her some food every Saturday, and I used to talk to all the ladies. They used to send-- you know, save for me chestnuts, because they liked me. I always used to come like the Little Red Riding Hood with the-- with the food for her grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, grandma was about 89 or so, 90, like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What became of her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was very lucky, because grandmother died a natural death about a week, or maybe it was a couple of weeks, before they-- the German came to the nursing home. They murdered all the-- all the people in the nursing home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was really lucky that she got buried in her all-- I don't know whether you know what tachrichim are. This is special clothes that you bury religious people. And she also had the little bag of-- of-- sand that was sent to her from Israel. Buried with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to go to the burial?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't-- I-- I didn't go. I-- I guess in Poland there was a custom that if you have your parents you weren't supposed to go to a funeral.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a Polish or a Jewish custom?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a Jewish custom. When you have living parents you're not supposed to go to the cemetery. Even when they say the holy prayer, when there was Yizkor, you used to-- you have to get out, because-- if you had living parents. Now, they don't believe this anymore. They tell you you can stay on even there is the [INAUDIBLE], the prayers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother go and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah. She-- she did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were still able to leave the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- the-- the-- the cemetery was in the ghetto. Was in a part-- was called, I think, [NON-ENGLISH]. I-- I'm not sure. But it was in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the nursing home in the ghetto as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were able to maintain kosher--

speaker: interviewee
text: They did.

speaker: interviewer
text: --while in the nursing home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But she didn't stay too long there because she passed on, which was really lucky for her. She-- at least we knew where she was buried.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school, yes. I went to school for a short while. And then they just closed all the schools, and everybody had to go to work. So they send me to a place. They were making carpets. It was a [NON-ENGLISH] resort. It consisted-- we had like a frame with some kind of a cotton going through. And we used to get pieces of material and make long, like, long pieces of material, and then we used to weave it in and make designs. They really came out quite nice. The only thing, sometimes we got stuff from people that were evacuated, and we saw there, like, three shirts one into the other. Because a lot of people, when they were shipped out of the ghetto, would wear a couple of things, you know, one on top of the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they were undressing and were taking away the clothes, one was in the other. And one of the women recognized her cousin's clothes. So we knew that the people that were evacuated, there was really-- probably weren't alive anymore. You told me that some of the clothing was bloodstained.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, some of the clothing was bloodstained. The time in the ghetto was the worst part of-- of our lives, because we lived in constant fear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: We never knew when there's going to be an appell and when they're going to take people off the streets. You were never sure where you're going to be when they're going to catch you. And that's what happened to my father. He was once walking in the streets, and they caught him on a truck, and they-- with a lot of other people, and he had no chance. And they just evacuated them and shipped them. We never heard of them again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where they shipped him to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we don't. We don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and he was gone, and it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was gone, and this was the last I ever heard of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was you and your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was me and my mother that was left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remain in the same place that you had originally been living?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stood there for a little while, and then they decided they have to concentrate people in-- in smaller places because they need more room. And they put me and my mother into another-- couple with a daughter, in their apartment. So they gave us a bed on the side. And they hated us, like it was our fault that they put us into their houses. But it really, you know, was not our fault. We didn't want to leave our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans put you into--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, yeah. They-- this was really the Jewish government on the instruction of the Germans, because they told them what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say the Jewish government, the government that existed within the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, yes. There was a government. There was policemen. There was-- there was, you know, a whole body. Like, the president was Rumkowski, and he has a lot of people. Like, my father worked for them, too, before they caught him. And no matter what he said, they just-- you couldn't talk to anybody. They just put you in a truck and just evacuated you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the 'theys' were--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were the people that were issuing all kinds of rules that we had to obey, even though the-- it was just the German that told the Jews what to do. So we had to go to the other people's apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really can't recall. But it--

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was a father, mother, and a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: And a daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: A daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were very mean to us. They put us-- our bed on the place where there was-- the water was coming down when it rains. And once my mother and me had to sleep with an umbrella, holding the umbrella up. And it was really very tough times. Because every time that the people came, that we heard that the Germans are coming to look for people, we used to hide in that little-- there was a little tiny-- it was like a little house but with one room, more like a storage room. Then me and my mom used to hide when we heard that the Germans were coming. So we were laying underneath a headboard under the window. So when the Germans would look in, they wouldn't see us. So that's how we were hiding, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the people that you were staying with eventually get taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that, because we left before that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I told you, I was-- I was working in the carpet factory. And then after a short while, they disbanded it and sent all the young children to a other factory, in a factory that were makings, I think, believe, was uniforms for the Germans, and we worked there. And from there, there was-- the German ask the director of this factory occasionally to give him names, people they should evacuate. And one day, there was a list, and my name was on this list. And I knew that I have to hide unless they come and-- and look for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had an uncle which was a policeman in the ghetto. So when I saw the name, I went to him and I asked him, please, could you do something? You know, the director of the place, they should take off my name from the list. And he did, and he managed to take off my name. But unfortunately for me, my name was typed twice, and my-- the other name was still on this list.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day they came to look for me. I was on-- we were in this little place that we were. This was a-- a couple of apartment were spread out, and we had a little bit of ground in the back. And all the tenants used to divide a little piece of ground, and we used to put some-- grow some vegetables-- radishes, beets, cucumbers. And we also had three fruit trees, which really helped us in the ghetto. But so we used to divide whatever fruit would grow against the tenants, and everybody got a little bit. And one day when I was in the back there, I heard somebody come in and yell, Eva Zysman. And I had a bad feeling about it because I knew my name was on the list. So I ran in the back, and I lay down between the-- the beet-- the beets, and I covered myself with the beets.

speaker: interviewer
text: The beet leaves were big enough to cover you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I put it all around. And they came to my mother and ask her, where is Eva? And she says, I don't know. I don't know where she is. So they said, well, we're going to wait. Says, I don't know how long it's going to take when she comes. But she says, here-- she gave him a bag of apples. Please write down that she's not here. So OK, they took the apples. They went away. And this was the first time that-- to save me.
